title: Macramé hammock
subtitle: A single-person hammock in 5 mm 3-ply cotton rope, 180 × 80 cm woven area with gathered ends
excerpt: A full-size single hammock built from a dense grid of alternating square knots, worked from two wooden spreader bars. 5 mm 3-ply cotton rope. Woven area 180 × 80 cm. A large, time-intensive project that requires a strong overhead mounting point while working.
sourceNotes: Macramé hammock construction using spreader bars and alternating square knots is established practice in rope-craft and macramé making. Dimensions, cord calculations, and construction sequence are original. Not drawn from a specific published source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A grid of square knots where each successive row is offset by two cords, creating an open diamond lattice. The standard macramé fabric stitch.

A finishing knot in which one cord winds repeatedly around a bundle of cords, then the tail is threaded through the wraps and pulled tight to lock them in place. Used to gather cord ends at spreader bars and ring connections.
A rigid bar (here, a 90 cm wooden dowel) used at each end of a hammock to hold the woven panel open to its full width and prevent it from collapsing into a tube.
The load-bearing rope threaded through the drilled holes at each end of the spreader bars and tied to the hanging points. Must be rated for the intended weight load.
A symmetrical flat knot made by crossing two working cords alternately left-over-right then right-over-left around a pair of filler cords. The structural unit of macramé fabric.

content.text: This hammock is a full-size single-person project built from a dense grid of
alternating square knots
worked in 5 mm 3-ply cotton cord. The woven area is 180 cm long by 80 cm wide, held open by two wooden
spreader bars
: 90 cm wooden dowels with holes drilled at each end through which the
suspension ropes
are threaded. Cord ends gather at each bar with multiple
wrapping knots

. Total cord requirement is approximately 120 m (40 cords, each cut to 3 m). This is a large, time-intensive project. Before starting, identify a strong overhead mounting point capable of holding the weight of a person: a ceiling joist hook, outdoor tree bracket, or workshop beam. The hammock is knotted vertically on that point, not on a board. Confirm that every
square knot
is consistently tied before committing to 120 m of 5 mm rope.

items.name: 5 mm 3-ply cotton cord — 40 cords, each cut to 3 m
Suspension rope, 10 mm natural hemp or cotton, rated to at least 150 kg — for threading through spreader bar holes and tying to hanging points
Wooden dowel, 90 cm long, 20 mm diameter, with 12 mm holes drilled 2 cm in from each end — spreader bars
Overhead ceiling hook or sturdy outdoor hanging bracket — for working and for permanent hanging
Macramé T-pins and board — for keeping knots aligned during lower sections
Cord measuring tape (for pre-measuring long lengths)

content.text: Cord cutting

Cut 40 cords of 5 mm 3-ply cotton cord, each 3 m long. Working with 5 mm rope at this length is physically demanding. A cord winder or reel stand prevents the bundle from knotting while you work. Do not attempt to bundle multiple cords into one cut; measure and cut each cord individually. Total: 120 m. An extra 5 m on hand is useful for wrapping knots at the gathered ends and for the suspension rope work, so purchase 125 m.
Setting up

Thread the suspension rope through the drilled hole at each end of the first spreader bar. Tie an overhand knot on each outer end so the rope cannot pull back through the hole. Bring both rope ends together above the bar and tie them to a ceiling hook or carabiner at working height. The bar should hang at roughly chest level so the cords hang freely below without touching the floor.

Fold each of the 40 cords in half and mount them onto the first spreader bar using lark's head knots, spacing them evenly across the 80 cm width. You will have 80 working tails hanging below. Group the tails into 20 sets of 4; each set of 4 forms one column of square knots.
The pattern

definition.caption: Square knot: two working cords around two filler cords, left-over-right then right-over-left

content.text: Rows 1 to 3: Setting the grid

Row 1: Starting at the left, tie one square knot in each group of 4 cords across the full width: 20 knots. Work at a consistent tension; the knots should sit 2 cm below the underside of the spreader bar. Row 2: Offset by two cords. Leave the first 2 tails free at the left edge. Group cords 3 to 6 as the first set, tie a square knot. Continue in groups of 4 across the width, leaving the last 2 tails free. 19 knots total. Row 3: Return to the full-width grouping: 20 knots, as Row 1. These three rows establish the alternating grid structure that continues for the length of the hammock.
Continuing to 180 cm

Continue alternating between full-width rows (20 knots) and offset rows (19 knots), measuring the panel length from the underside of the spreader bar after every 10 rows. In 5 mm 3-ply cotton at normal macramé tension, 10 rows of alternating square knots produces roughly 8 to 10 cm of panel. Adjust tension consistently to keep rows level. Continue until the panel measures 180 cm below the spreader bar.
Edge cords

The two edge tails on each side are left free in offset rows. Incorporate them back into a half-knot at the edge on every other row to create a firm, defined selvedge. Without this, the hammock edge will have a loose, ladder-like margin. A half-square knot using just the outer two cords and the adjacent two tails is sufficient. Tie it snug against the previous row before moving to the interior knots.
Finishing
Attaching the second spreader bar

Once the panel reaches 180 cm, bring the second spreader bar below the last row. Working left to right, tie the first 4 tails to the bar with a wrapping knot: hold the 4 tails against the bar, use a separate 50 cm length of 5 mm cord to wind tightly around the bundle and bar eight to ten times, then thread the tail of the wrapping cord through the final loop and pull it through the wraps to lock. Repeat across all 40 cord groups: 20 bundles wrapped to the second bar. This gathers the panel tails to the bar and fixes the lower end.
Suspension rope for the lower bar

Thread a 2 m length of suspension rope through each drilled hole in the second spreader bar, knotting the inner ends. Bring both ends down and together to form a loop below the bar; tie them at the desired distance from the bar with an overhand knot. This loop attaches to the lower hanging point. The total hanging length from point to point should be 250 to 300 cm to allow comfortable use at seated height.
Weight test

Before using the hammock, test it with a dead load: hang a bag of comparable weight (60 to 80 kg) in the hammock for 30 minutes and inspect every wrapping knot and suspension rope tie for slipping. Check that the spreader bar holes have not split or cracked. 3-ply cotton rope will stretch slightly under load on first use. Re-tension the suspension ropes after the first session.

items.problem: The panel is narrowing toward the lower end
items.cause: Knot tension increasing row by row as the weight of the panel pulls the working cords inward
items.fix: Measure width every 20 rows. If narrowing: slightly loosen the working cord pulls so each knot is set to the intended 4 cm lateral spacing. Pin the outer tails to a board behind the panel.

items.problem: The wrapping knots at the spreader bars are slipping
items.cause: Insufficient wraps or tail not properly locked through the coils
items.fix: Add a second wrapping knot alongside the first, or thread the locking tail through the coils twice in opposite directions before pulling tight. 3-ply cotton does not grip as firmly as single-twist; tight wraps are essential.

items.problem: Cord running out before the panel reaches 180 cm
items.cause: Tighter-than-expected knot tension consuming cord faster
items.fix: Join new cord by overlapping 10 cm with the short tail, holding both tails behind the work and knotting over them for the next 4 knots. Trim the overlap to 2 cm after locking. The join will not be visible from the front under normal use.

items.problem: The panel rows are not level
items.cause: Tension inconsistency between the left and right sides of the panel
items.fix: Place a T-pin at the row height on both edges after every fifth row and align the row against the pins before tightening. Work from the centre outward rather than left to right.
